narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 24, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your lower decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 grades in school. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far higher than what it sees in most applicants, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure risk stems from a few key aspects of your profile that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest influence was your Year 3 school decile ranking at the bottom (1st decile, compared to the typical 5th or 6th decile), which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours often signal challenges in sustaining performance through law school. Next, your LSAT score of 24—well below the average of around 37—reinforced this concern, pulling the odds even further toward failure based on how similar scores have historically correlated with bar struggles. Your Year 1 decile at the bottom also contributed in the same direction, though with somewhat less weight, highlighting early academic hurdles that the model links to later bar outcomes. Compared to typical students who get approved, your combination of low early and later school deciles plus the LSAT score deviates sharply from their stronger averages, tipping the scales against you despite your full-time commitment and solid family income bracket. While factors like your background as a Hispanic male weren't the main drivers here, they fit into the broader patterns the model considers. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—it's just the model's data-driven read on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted tutoring for the academic weaknesses shown in your deciles. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value persistence like yours. Keep pushing; many in similar situations have turned it around with focused improvements.
